updates.title: Claude Sonnet 4.6 — New Default Model
updates.excerpt: Anthropic releases Claude Sonnet 4.6 as the new default for Claude Code. Faster, cheaper, and measurably better on SWE-bench and real-world coding benchmarks than 4.5.
updates.content: Claude Sonnet 4.6 is now the recommended model for Claude Code. It delivers improved performance on complex multi-file edits, better instruction following in long agentic sessions, and lower latency. Set it explicitly in settings.json: { "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6" }. Existing sessions auto-upgrade on next launch.

updates.title: Fast Mode — Toggle Speed on Opus Mid-Session
updates.excerpt: Claude Code now supports /fast toggle on Opus 4.6 — same quality output, significantly faster generation. Switch mid-session without losing context.
updates.content: Fast mode activates a streaming-optimised inference path on Opus 4.6. Toggle with /fast on or /fast off at any point in a session. Context, tool state, and conversation history are fully preserved. Particularly useful when you want Opus reasoning for architecture but faster output for implementation steps.

updates.title: Auto-Memory — MEMORY.md Persists Across Sessions
updates.excerpt: Claude Code now auto-reads MEMORY.md files at session start, giving persistent project memory without any manual context-loading.
updates.content: Place a MEMORY.md at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md and Claude reads it automatically every session. Organise by topic: Architecture, Key File Paths, Gotchas, User Preferences. No more re-explaining your stack at the start of each session. Combine with CLAUDE.md for zero-warmup sessions.

updates.title: Parallel Sub-Agents — Multiple Agents in One Response
updates.excerpt: Claude Code can now invoke multiple Agent tool calls in a single response, running them simultaneously. Test scan + security scan + docs generation in one shot.
updates.content: When tasks are independent, Claude now fans out multiple sub-agents simultaneously rather than sequentially. A single instruction like 'run tests, security scan, and update docs' spawns three parallel agents. Results are merged back into the main context. Typical speedup: 3-5x for multi-task workflows. Enable via { "agents": { "parallel": true } } in settings.json.

updates.title: MEMORY.md + Skills = Zero Warm-Up Time Every Session
updates.excerpt: Engineer shares how combining MEMORY.md (persistent project facts) with Skills (procedural know-how) eliminates all context-loading at session start. Cold sessions now feel like warm ones.
updates.content: User 'zero_warmup_dev' posted a detailed breakdown: MEMORY.md holds WHO (architecture, key paths, non-obvious decisions), Skills hold HOW (how to deploy, how to run tests, how to migrate DB). CLAUDE.md holds rules. With all three in place, Claude Code sessions start fully context-aware with zero manual re-explanation. Template shared in comments.

updates.title: One CLAUDE.md Trick That Saved Our Team 200 Hours
updates.excerpt: A senior engineer shares how adding a 10-line 'Verify Changes' section to their CLAUDE.md eliminated all regression bugs introduced by Claude across a 6-month project.
updates.content: User 'devguru_42' posted a detailed breakdown showing how adding explicit verification steps in CLAUDE.md (run linter, run tests, check types) reduced Claude-introduced regressions to zero. The key insight: Claude follows verification steps in CLAUDE.md much more reliably than inline instructions. Template shared in comments, 2.4k upvotes.

updates.title: Multi-Agent Pipeline That Deploys to Production Automatically
updates.excerpt: Developer shares a 3-agent pipeline: planner → implementer → reviewer. The reviewer agent must approve before any commit. Zero human intervention for routine features.
updates.content: User 'pipeline_wizard' detailed a production multi-agent setup: (1) planner-agent.md uses Opus to create implementation plan, (2) implementer-agent.md uses Sonnet to execute it, (3) reviewer-agent.md uses Sonnet with Read-only access to approve or reject. On approval, a hook auto-commits and pushes. Running for 3 months with 94% approval rate.

updates.title: Checkpoints — Save & Roll Back Instantly
updates.excerpt: One of the most-requested features lands: Checkpoints let you save Claude Code session state and roll back to any previous point with a single command.
updates.content: Checkpoints address the frustration of long agentic sessions going off-track. Run /checkpoint save <name> at any point, and /checkpoint restore <name> to instantly revert all file changes made since that save point. Checkpoints persist across sessions. Ideal for exploratory refactoring and risky migrations.

updates.title: Token Optimization Tricks That Actually Work (2026)
updates.excerpt: Community-curated list of token-saving techniques from power users — from CLAUDE.md trimming to MCP server management. Verified with /cost measurements.
updates.content: Top-voted thread: (1) Skills instead of CLAUDE.md for procedures saves ~15k tokens/session, (2) Disabling unused MCP servers saves 500-2000 tokens each, (3) Targeted file reads instead of letting Claude explore saves 1-5k per session, (4) /compact after each major task saves 5-20k, (5) Using Haiku for boilerplate saves 80% on those tasks.

updates.title: Claude Code Replaced My Entire Code Review Process
updates.excerpt: Tech lead explains how they retired their manual PR review checklist in favor of a code-reviewer agent that catches more issues in 10 seconds than humans catch in 30 minutes.
updates.content: Highly upvoted post from 'cto_of_startup': their code-reviewer.md agent now runs on every PR via GitHub Actions. Average review time dropped from 45 minutes to 90 seconds. Human reviewers now focus exclusively on architecture and business logic.

updates.title: Agentless: Demystifying LLM-based Software Engineering Agents
updates.abstract: Agentless challenges the assumption that complex agent frameworks are needed for software engineering tasks. It shows a simple two-phase approach — fault localisation then repair — matches or beats complex agent systems on SWE-bench, at a fraction of the cost and complexity.
updates.excerpt: Simple 2-phase approach (localise → repair) matches complex agent frameworks on SWE-bench at far lower cost. Key lesson for Claude Code users: don't over-engineer your agent pipelines.

updates.title: SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
updates.abstract: SWE-agent introduces Agent-Computer Interfaces (ACIs) — specialised tools that make it dramatically easier for LLMs to browse files, run tests, and edit code. The paper shows the interface design matters as much as the model itself.
updates.excerpt: The tools you give Claude matter as much as the model. Designing lean, purposeful interfaces (like limiting to Read/Grep/Edit) outperforms giving unrestricted bash access.

updates.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
updates.authors: Yao et al., Google Brain / Princeton
updates.abstract: ReAct interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with action steps — letting the model think before each tool call. This reduces hallucinations and improves task completion on multi-step agent workflows significantly over pure action or pure reasoning approaches.
updates.excerpt: The pattern behind Claude Code's think-then-act loop. Understanding ReAct explains why writing detailed step-by-step instructions in CLAUDE.md consistently outperforms vague high-level prompts.

updates.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
updates.abstract: Toolformer shows LLMs can learn when and how to call external APIs (calculators, search, calendars) in a self-supervised way. The paper demonstrates that well-formatted tool descriptions dramatically improve tool call accuracy.
updates.excerpt: Why clear, consistent tool descriptions in your SKILL.md and agent files lead to far more reliable tool use. Claude was trained on this pattern — write your skill files to exploit it.

updates.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
updates.abstract: Constitutional AI is the foundational technique behind Claude's safety properties. The model critiques and revises its own outputs against a set of principles. Understanding this helps Claude Code users write better system prompts and CLAUDE.md constraints.
updates.excerpt: The technique behind Claude's guardrails. Writing your CLAUDE.md 'Do Not' rules in the same constitutional style — clear, specific, reason-giving — measurably improves Claude's compliance.
